title: PTD Today — Media
disclaimer: Informational only — AI-generated summaries; may contain errors. Verify with the original video.

items.title: AWS Power Hour: Generative AI For Developers | S1 E1 | Exam Overview and FM Integrations

ai.summary: Based on the available description, the hosts break down the exam format, share study strategies, and guide listeners through designing resilient GenAI architectures. They cover building data processing pipelines for foundation models, implementing vector stores for RAG systems, applying prompt engineering strategies, and analyze sample exam questions with real-world solutions for advanced GenAI systems.
bullets: Overview of exam format and study strategies for GenAI-related assessments
Guidance on designing resilient generative AI architectures
How to build data processing pipelines tailored for foundation models
Implementing vector stores for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems
Prompt engineering techniques plus analysis of sample exam questions and real-world solutions
takeaways: Resilient GenAI architectures imply a need for infrastructure planning to ensure availability and fault tolerance for model hosting
Data pipelines for foundation models will demand scalable throughput and storage considerations in data center planning
Vector stores for RAG systems require low-latency storage and retrieval capabilities that affect storage architecture and networking
Prompt engineering and exam-focused skills point to investment in staff training and hiring for GenAI-specific competencies

Because no transcript or detailed claims are provided, verify capabilities and compliance through official documentation and demos.

items.title: The Most Expensive Mistake in AI (And How to Avoid It)

ai.summary: Based on the available description Marcus Oliver, lead of Google delta (Google’s professional services team), argues the most expensive mistake in AI is rushing to code without a strategy rather than focusing on computing costs. He advises organizations to escape the “coding trap” by reimagining value chains and deploying multi-agent systems to deliver real business outcomes.
bullets: Most expensive AI mistake: rushing to code without a strategy, not compute costs.
Marcus Oliver (Google delta lead) identifies the “coding trap” as a core problem.
Focus on reimagining value chains before building solutions.
Deploy multi-agent systems to drive real business outcomes.
Related resources: The AI Vantage series and Google Cloud links provided.
takeaways: Prioritize clear AI strategy and business alignment before committing infrastructure or projects.
Don’t treat compute costs as the primary failure point—ensure the use case and value chain are defined first.
Assess data center and grid investments for their ability to support multi-agent, outcome-focused workloads.
Engage professional services or experts to map AI initiatives to measurable business outcomes before heavy build-out.

ai.summary: Based on the available description, this replay episode from PwC’s Reporting reset series reviews foundational principles for financial statement presentation, including GAAP and SEC requirements, reporting periods, balance sheet and income statement presentation, accounting changes, and subsequent events. The episode provides timestamps for each topic, links to relevant chapters in PwC’s Financial statement presentation guide, and features PwC National Office speakers.
bullets: Covers foundational GAAP and SEC presentation requirements (1:25)
Guidance on determining appropriate reporting periods (3:09)
Balance sheet presentation: classification, disclosures, best practices (5:25)
Income statement structure and considerations (11:44); accounting changes, estimates, and error corrections (21:31)
Subsequent events: recognition and disclosure (29:53) and links to PwC guide chapters
takeaways: Ensure year‑end reporting aligns with foundational GAAP and applicable SEC presentation and disclosure requirements.
Confirm and document appropriate reporting periods and how they are presented in financial statements.
Review balance sheet and income statement classifications and required disclosures to support transparent external reporting.
Track and disclose accounting changes, estimate shifts, error corrections, and subsequent events per guidance and the linked PwC chapters.

items.title: DeepKeep: Secure your AI agents and applications end-to-end

ai.summary: Based on the available description, DeepKeep on Google Cloud is a unified platform designed to secure AI agents, applications, and user interactions by providing visibility, policy controls, and real-time protections. It highlights AI Lens for interaction visibility and policy enforcement, AI Red Teaming for stress-testing models and apps, and an AI Firewall to secure prompts and responses.
bullets: Unified platform to secure AI agents, applications, and user interactions.
AI Lens: visibility into AI interactions and policy application to eliminate shadow AI.
AI Red Teaming: stress-tests applications and models against realistic attacks.
AI Firewall: real-time prompt/response protection with built-in guardrails.
Aims to protect infrastructure from evolving AI threats.
takeaways: Consolidated AI security can reduce operational blind spots and shadow AI risk.
Proactive red-teaming helps identify vulnerabilities before they impact production systems.
Real-time prompt/response controls lower the chance of runtime compromise or data leakage.
A single platform approach simplifies policy enforcement across agents, apps, and users.

Concise, promotional tone with a star emoji (🌟).
No transcript available; description is the sole content source.
takeaways: Messaging positions Copilot as an empowering, user-facing tool—expect user interest driven by emotive marketing.
A visible call to action for trials suggests decision-makers should plan how pilot access, governance, and compliance will be handled.
Prepare support and internal guidance in case promotional outreach generates requests to adopt or test Copilot within teams.

items.title: GeForce Garage - Summer of RTX Community Build

ai.summary: Based on the available description… NVIDIA's GeForce Garage video spotlights the winning r/buildapc Summer of RTX community build: a small-form-factor PC featuring a GeForce RTX 5090 Founders Edition. The description lists full system specs, including a Fractal Design Era 2 chassis, Ryzen 7 9800X3D, MSI MPG B850I Edge motherboard, 32GB DDR5-6000 RAM, Corsair SF1000 power supply, and Corsair Nautilus 240 RS AIO.
bullets: Winner of the Summer of RTX r/buildapc contest: u/JeffersonPutnam.
SFF build uses Fractal Design Era 2 chassis with GeForce RTX 5090 Founders Edition GPU.
Key components listed: Ryzen 7 9800X3D, MSI MPG B850I Edge, 32GB Teamgroup DDR5-6000.
Power and cooling: Corsair SF1000 power supply and Corsair Nautilus 240 RS AIO.
Video hosted by GeForce host Dwight (Twitter: @_MightyPotato) with full PCPartPicker parts list linked.
takeaways: Demonstrates NVIDIA promoting the RTX 5090 in consumer community builds, signaling vendor-supported showcase use cases.
Provides a concrete example of pairing a high-end RTX 5090 with a compact SFF chassis and a 1000-series SFX power supply (Corsair SF1000).
Lists a modern CPU/motherboard/RAM combination (Ryzen 7 9800X3D + MSI B850I + DDR5-6000) that users are deploying alongside top-tier GPUs.
Includes mainstream SFF cooling and power choices (Corsair Nautilus 240 RS AIO and Corsair SF1000), useful reference for compact-system power/thermal provisioning.
Highlights community engagement (r/buildapc contest) as a channel NVIDIA uses to surface real-world builds and component combinations.
